title: Number Representation & Place Value
objective: Students will explore number representation & place value concepts including A Hundred Is Ten Tens, The three digits of a three-digit number, Ordering Numbers to 1000 and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about A Hundred Is Ten Tens. Get ready for an adventure!
Understand that 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens — called a 'hundred'
Let me show you something cool. Explain that 10 groups of 10 ones make 100
Can you explain that ten full tens stacked together make one hundred — and that this is why 100 has a 1 in the hundreds place and zeros in the rest?
You did it! Here's what we explored about A Hundred Is Ten Tens: Explain that 10 groups of 10 ones make 100 Bundle ten tens sticks into one hundred and describe what happened

lessons.title: The three digits of a three-digit number

steps.content: Let's discover the amazing world of The three digits of a three-digit number!
Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones
Let me show you something cool. State the value of each digit in a three-digit number (e.g. in 362, the 3 represents 3 hundreds)
If you sees the number 348, can they tell you there are 3 hundreds, 4 tens, and 8 ones — rather than just reading the digits as 'three four eight'?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about The three digits of a three-digit number. State the value of each digit in a three-digit number (e.g. in 362, the 3 represents 3 hundreds) Partition a three-digit number into hundreds, tens, and ones (e.g. 485 = 400 + 80 + 5)
Let's explore Ordering Numbers to 1000! Have you ever wondered about this?
Compare and order numbers up to 1000 using >, =, and < symbols, based on place-value understanding
Let's practice together! Compare two three-digit numbers by examining hundreds first, then tens, then ones
If you give you a set of three-digit numbers like 456, 231, and 789, can they put them in order and use > and < correctly?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Ordering Numbers to 1000: Compare two three-digit numbers by examining hundreds first, then tens, then ones Use >, =, and < correctly to record comparisons of three-digit numbers
Today we're going to learn about The multiples of 100. Get ready for an adventure!
Understand that the multiples of 100 (100–900) each represent a number of hundreds with 0 tens and 0 ones
Time to try this out: Identify that 300 means 3 hundreds, 0 tens, 0 ones
Can you explain that 700 means 7 hundreds with no tens and no ones — and show what that looks like using base-ten blocks or a drawing?
Great job! Today we learned about The multiples of 100. Identify that 300 means 3 hundreds, 0 tens, 0 ones Place multiples of 100 on a number line to 1000
Let's discover the amazing world of Reading and writing numbers to 1000!
Read and write numbers to 1000 in numerals, number names, and expanded form
Time to try this out: Read a three-digit numeral aloud correctly
Can you read and write numbers up to 1,000 — both as numerals and in words — like reading '642' as 'six hundred and forty-two'?
Great job! Today we learned about Reading and writing numbers to 1000. Read a three-digit numeral aloud correctly Write a three-digit number in words (e.g. 'three hundred and forty-two')
Let's discover the amazing world of 10 or 100 More or Less!
Find 10 or 100 more or less than a given number up to 1000
Time to try this out: Given a three-digit number, state what is 10 more and 10 less
Can you quickly work out '10 more than 342' or '100 less than 875' in their head — knowing which digit changes and which stays the same?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about 10 or 100 More or Less. Given a three-digit number, state what is 10 more and 10 less Given a three-digit number, state what is 100 more and 100 less
Have you ever thought about Place Value to 1000? Let's find out together!
Solve number problems and practical problems involving place value of numbers up to 1000
Time to try this out: Solve a problem that requires identifying how many hundreds, tens, or ones are in a number
If you is told 'I have 4 hundreds, 3 tens, and 7 ones — what number is that?', can they put it together and tell you 437?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Place Value to 1000. Solve a problem that requires identifying how many hundreds, tens, or ones are in a number Apply place-value knowledge to a practical context (e.g. counting money in pounds)
Let's discover the amazing world of Odd or Even!
Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members
Let's practice together! Pair objects and determine whether there is one left over (odd) or not (even)
If you has a group of 14 objects, can they tell you whether that number is odd or even — for example by pairing them up to see if any are left over?
Great job! Today we learned about Odd or Even. Pair objects and determine whether there is one left over (odd) or not (even) Count a group by 2s to determine if the total is even
